summary:
The Persian conquest of Egypt (525 BC) by Cambyses II, son of [[event:1911|Cyrus the Great]], ended the Saite dynasty and incorporated Egypt into the Achaemenid Empire as its richest satrapy. The decisive battle at Pelusium on the northeastern frontier of Egypt was reportedly won partly through a psychological trick: Cambyses arranged for cats (sacred animals in Egypt) to be placed in front of his advancing soldiers, paralyzing Egyptian archers who feared killing the sacred animals. Whether true or legendary, Egypt fell quickly.

Cambyses' treatment of Egypt is remembered harshly in later Greek and Egyptian sources — Herodotus accused him of killing the sacred Apis bull, mocking Egyptian religion, and general impiety and madness. Modern scholarship views this tradition skeptically: the Apis bull may have died of natural causes, and Cambyses did make offerings at Egyptian temples. However, he did destroy temples in Karnak and other sites, suggesting a less tolerant approach than his father Cyrus. Egypt remained a Persian satrapy (with intermittent native revolts and a period of independence 404–343 BC) until Alexander the Great's conquest in 332 BC, and Persian pharaohs took Egyptian royal titles and made religious dedications in an attempt to establish legitimacy.
